books.title: Designing Data-Intensive Applications
books.author: Martin Kleppmann
books.note: The best systems book I've read. Every chapter reframes how you think about data at scale. Kleppmann has a rare gift for making distributed systems feel intuitive.

books.title: The Pragmatic Programmer
books.author: Andrew Hunt & David Thomas
books.note: Timeless advice on the craft of software. Every tip feels hard-won.

books.title: Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs
books.author: Harold Abelson & Gerald Jay Sussman

papers.title: MapReduce: Simplified Data Processing on Large Clusters
papers.note: The paper that made parallelism feel obvious in hindsight. The genius is in what it leaves out — the programming model is deliberately constrained to make automatic parallelization tractable.

papers.title: Dynamo: Amazon's Highly Available Key-Value Store
papers.note: A masterclass in pragmatic distributed systems design. The explicit trade-off of consistency for availability shaped how I think about SLA-driven architecture decisions.

papers.title: In Search of an Understandable Consensus Algorithm
papers.note: Raft is designed for understandability, and it delivers. After reading this, Paxos finally clicked too — sometimes the best way to understand something is to see a cleaner alternative.
